title: Onboarding Materials in Half the Time

sections.content:
**Onboarding That Protects the Hiring Investment**

The cost of a bad hire is 1.5–2x annual salary. Yet most companies spend weeks or months on hiring and days on onboarding. Onboarding that's vague, inconsistent, or overwhelming is one of the most common contributors to early attrition.

AI makes it practical to create role-specific, high-quality onboarding materials without the weeks of documentation work it usually requires.

**What effective onboarding materials actually do:**
- Reduce time-to-productivity by eliminating the time new hires spend figuring out things that could be documented
- Set clear expectations for what success looks like at 30, 60, and 90 days
- Help new hires build relationships with the right people faster
- Communicate culture through specifics, not platitudes ('we have a feedback culture' means nothing; 'we do monthly structured 1:1s and have a written feedback protocol' does)

**Types of onboarding content AI helps produce:**
- **Role-specific first-week checklists** — what to set up, who to meet, what to read, what to deliver
- **Team and company orientation guides** — how we work, key meetings, tools, decision-making norms
- **30/60/90 day success frameworks** — what does good look like at each milestone?
- **FAQ documents** — answers to the questions every new hire asks in month 1
- **Stakeholder maps** — who this role interacts with and what they care about

**The inputs AI needs:**
The more specific your inputs, the more useful the output. Don't ask AI to write a generic onboarding guide — give it your actual tools, your real meeting cadences, and your specific team structure. The best onboarding materials are specific enough that they couldn't have been written for a different company.

sections.bad: Write an onboarding guide for a new marketing hire.
sections.good:
Write an onboarding guide for a new Content Marketing Manager joining a 40-person B2B SaaS company. Here's the specific context:

**Tools they'll use:** Notion (content calendar and docs), Webflow (publishing), Semrush (SEO), HubSpot (lead tracking and email), Slack (#content, #marketing, #general channels)

**Team structure:** Reports to VP Marketing. Works closely with 2 other marketers (demand gen and product marketing). Main internal stakeholders: Sales team, Product team for launch coordination

**Key meetings:** Weekly team standup (Mon 9am), Monthly all-hands (first Thu), Quarterly planning (with VP Marketing + CEO), Bi-weekly 1:1 with VP Marketing

**What success looks like at 30 days:** Understands existing content strategy, has audited the last 6 months of content performance, has a relationship with the 3 sales reps they'll work most closely with

**What success looks like at 90 days:** Has published 4 pieces independently, has proposed a content calendar for Q3, has identified at least one underperforming content type to sunset or revamp

Please produce: (1) a first-week checklist with daily structure, (2) a 30/60/90 day success framework, and (3) a 'how we work' section that explains the team cadences and decision-making norms.
sections.explanation: The vague prompt produces a generic onboarding template from a training manual. The specific prompt gives AI real tools, real meetings, real stakeholders, and real success criteria — the output will be immediately usable rather than requiring complete rewriting. The first-week checklist and 30/60/90 framework will reflect how this company actually works.

sections.prompt: Put these onboarding content creation steps in the correct order for creating effective, role-specific onboarding materials using AI.

steps: Have AI draft the onboarding materials from your notes
Collect the role-specific inputs: tools, key meetings, stakeholders, 30/60/90 success criteria
Have recent new hires review the draft and flag anything missing or confusing
Define what 'successful onboarding' looks like — what should this person be able to do at 30, 60, 90 days?
Finalize and publish the materials, with a plan for keeping them updated as the role or tools evolve
data.explanation: Start with the success definition — if you don't know what successful onboarding looks like, you can't build towards it. Then gather the specific inputs (tools, meetings, stakeholders) that make materials role-specific rather than generic. Give AI those inputs to draft the materials. Have recent new hires review — they are the best testers of whether the materials actually prepare someone for the role. Finally, publish with a maintenance plan, because outdated onboarding materials that reference tools no longer in use actively mislead new hires.

sections.prompt: Write a prompt to create a first-week onboarding checklist for a new hire in a role on your team. Include specific details about the role, tools, key people, and what the person should accomplish in their first week.

rubric: Strong prompts include: the role and team context, the specific tools the person will use, key meetings and their purpose, 3–5 people the new hire should meet and why, and clear first-week deliverables or milestones
They ask for a daily or structured format (not a flat list) and specify that the output should be specific enough to be immediately useful without modification
Weak prompts ask for a generic onboarding checklist for a job title without any company or role-specific context
data.exampleAnswer:
Write a first-week onboarding checklist for a new Customer Success Manager joining a B2B SaaS company. Here are the specifics:

**Role context:** The CSM owns a portfolio of 20 mid-market accounts (ARR $30k–$150k each). Primary focus in the first 90 days is learning the accounts and getting through their first round of quarterly business reviews.

**Tools to set up:** Salesforce (CRM), Gainsight (customer health scores), Slack (#cs-team, #product, #sales), Zoom, Notion (team wiki)

**Key people to meet in week 1:**
- Their manager (CSM Lead) — for role expectations and priority accounts
- Two experienced CSMs on the team — for shadow calls and account handoff context
- A rep from the Sales team — to understand how accounts come in and what's been promised
- A rep from the Product team — to understand the roadmap and how CSMs communicate it to customers

**First-week deliverables:**
- All tools set up and accounts listed in Salesforce
- Completed 2 shadow calls with an experienced CSM
- Read the last QBR deck for 3 assigned accounts
- Met all 4 key people above

Please structure this as a day-by-day checklist (Monday through Friday) that is specific and actionable — not a flat list. Include a 'by end of week 1' success summary.
